assignments.title: Project: Data Acquisition [82078.202580, 82079.202580]
assignments.description:
Unit 1 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser, hidden in app)

 

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The objective of this project is for students to acquire and display results from reading both structured and unstructured data from various sources, including raw files, SQL databases, online repositories, and programmatically through the use of APIs. This project aims to help students understand how to ingest data from diverse sources, focusing primarily on using the pandas library to interact with the data. Students will retrieve data from different sources, comprehend the differences between data types, and become familiar with Jupyter notebooks, a key tool for data science.

Key Focus Areas:

* Using Jupyter notebooks for data acquisition

* Utilizing pandas, SQL, and other important libraries to acquire data

* Understanding the differences between structured, unstructured, time series, and real-time data

* Recognizing the differences between primary and secondary data sources

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Download and use this [Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063903?wrap=1) for your solutions to the tasks below. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file, which you will submit.

Part 1: Structured Data
-----------------------

Data can be ingested in many different ways, Pandas offers a lot of ways to load data, see https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/io.html 

Pickling

Flat File 

Clipbard

Excel

JSON

HTML

XML

Latex

HDF5

Feather

Parquet

ORC

SAS

SPSS

SQL

BigQuery

STATA

 ... 

TASK: Your task is to read several different file types using pandas (e.g., csv, text, json, database) and learn about some pandas functionalities to display the loaded data. Instructions for each are provided below.

CSV
---

CSV File Acquisition
--------------------

* Load csv data from the provided data_url below, using pandas into a dataframe

data_url = "https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/wine-quality/winequality-red.csv"

* Print the dataframe's first few elements using the .head() command to see what data you have

* Change the names of the columns, using [df.rename](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.rename.html)

* Try this with inplace=true

* Try again without inplace=true but make sure to resave the dataframe

* Print the tail of the dataframe using the .tail() command

Text (TXT)
----------

TXT File Acquisition
--------------------

* Load [employees.txt] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063931?wrap=1) in using pandas into a dataframe

* Print the dataframe's first few elements using the .head() command to see what data you have

* Using the info, describe, value_counts, and groupby functions in pandas, please answer the following questions

* How many entries and columns are there in the dataset?

* What is the average age and average salary of the employees in the dataset?

* Which occupation has the highest number of employees?

* What is the average salary for each occupation, and which occupation has the highest average salary?

JSON
----

JSON File Acquisition
---------------------

* Load the [movies.json] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063757?wrap=1) file using pandas into a dataframe

* Print the dataframe's first few elements using the .head() command to see what data you have

* Use the isna() function to get a count of how many missing values there are in the dataframe

* Use the sample() function to understand its use

Database
--------

Database Acquisition (OPTIONAL for Advanced Users)
--------------------------------------------------

* Load the [employees.db] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063744?wrap=1) in using pandas read_sql() function and store in a dataframe

* Print the dataframe's first few elements using the .head() command to see what data you have

* Perform SQL queries to find the following information:

* Number of unique departments

* Average salary

* Number of employees

* Who are the employees in the engineering department?

Part 2: Unstructured Data
-------------------------

Data can be provided in various formats, with some examples beyond structured data being:

* Sometimes, you may need to access data held by another company. The best approach is to use an API to request the company's data services, which will return the necessary data.

* Sometimes, you may need to work with an entire directory of files. The best approach is to loop through the directory and read each file.

TASK: Your task is to practice using an API and reading a directory of text files to extract information. Detailed instructions for each method are provided below.

APIs
----

Application Programming Interfaces (mini project)
-------------------------------------------------

We will practice connecting to the SpaceX API and see what information we can get back!

* Connect to the SpaceX API - Set up a connection to the SpaceX API. We can use the requests library to connect to the SpaceX API by specifying the API endpoint URL.
More info at: https://docs.spacexdata.com/ 

* Retrieve Data on Past Launches - Fetch data on past launches from the SpaceX API. We can use the requests.get() method to send a GET request to the SpaceX API endpoint and use the .json() method to parse the response.

* Load Data into a Pandas DataFrame - Load the retrieved data into a Pandas DataFrame.

* Perform Basic Data Analysis - Perform basic data analysis on the retrieved data. We can use the describe() and info() methods to get basic statistics and information about the DataFrame.

* Filter Data Based on Launch Success - Filter the data to find only successful launches. We can use boolean indexing to filter the DataFrame for rows where the success column is True.

* Group Data by Year - Note, use the column "date_utc" in your dataset. Group the data by year and count the number of launches each year. We can use the pd.to_datetime() method to convert the date column to datetime, extract the year using the .dt.year accessor, and use the groupby() method to group the data by year and count the number of launches.

Directory of Files
------------------

Reading a Directory of Files
----------------------------

* Since we did not cover this, you will be provided the code to process of working with a directory of data. We will use a collection of text data (movie reviews) to demonstrate how to load, process, and analyze unstructured text data.

* Load Text Data from Files - See if you can setup the 'reviews' directory and read the three given text files (e.g., [review1.txt] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063812?wrap=1), [review2.txt] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063901?wrap=1), [review3.txt] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063926?wrap=1)). To load the data, we have to use typical python file loading.

* We will need the os package and have to loop through our files to find all .txt files.

* Once we find them, we will load them into an array for storage.

* And print them out to make sure we got all the files!

* As a stretch goal, modify the code and see if you can read other types of files.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file. To submit your notebook, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time, but for larger data sets in the future, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

assignments.title: Module 3 | Discussion [Fall 2025.MATH561A.81045]
assignments.description:
---------------------------------------------------------
On to classification, and right into logistic regression!
---------------------------------------------------------

In 560, you have explored and applied a lot of powerful techniques for settings where your response variable was quantitative. In settings where your response variable is qualitative, you need different tools, so called classification methods, and logistic regression is the main stay of all of them! Here you will get a chance to discuss the mathematical foundations and their implications for your work in some depth.

* What is the defining characteristic of a classification problem?

* Recall and explain what the issues are with using a standard linear model to use for modeling a classification problem.

* How does the logistical regression model differ from the linear regression model? How do you interpret the logistic regression predictions and the inferred parameters?

* Can cross-validation still be performed when we are dealing with a classification problem? Why or why not?

* Provide one example application of a classification problem in a field of your choice, and discuss why a regression model is a poor fit in your example.

 

Post your thoughts to the questions above and provide constructive feedback to at least two (2) of your peers.

You will in turn receive feedback from your colleagues and instructors, and have until EOD Sunday to address the feedback you receive from your colleagues and instructors before your discussion post will be graded.

Estimated time requirement: 3 hours

assignments.title: Module 4 | Discussion [Fall 2025.MATH561A.81045]
assignments.description:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Linear and Quadratic Discriminant Analysis - does it get any more complicated than that?
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

LDA and QDA are conceptually challenging methods and rely quite heavily on the assumption of normality of the predictor variables. While they are powerful methods in the right setting, you want to carefully check these assumptions when applying these methods in your work.

* Explain the goals of LDA and QDA. What do these two methods have in common and what is different?

* Select a publicly available dataset (a good source is https://catalog.data.gov/dataset?q=&sort=views_recent+desc) and analyze the data using LDA or QDA. Discuss your results and include plots or graphics. Be sure to mention whether the assumptions for these methods were met, which features you chose to analyze, and what the results were.

* What role does sample size play in your decision to use QDA vs LDA? Discuss this question in the context of number of parameters to estimate and how you would go about making the call which methods to use in your work.

 

Post your thoughts to the questions above and provide constructive feedback to at least two (2) of your peers.

You will in turn receive feedback from your colleagues and instructors, and have until EOD Sunday to address the feedback you receive from your colleagues and instructors before your discussion post will be graded.

Estimated time requirement: 4 hours

assignments.description:
---------------------------------------
To prune or not - that is the question!
---------------------------------------

Tree-pruning is an important step in using decision trees when analyzing data you will encounter in your career.

* The approach to decision trees described in the book is a greedy approach. Explain what is meant by the term "greedy". Describe why this algorithm to build trees does not always arrive at a globally optimal tree.

* Discuss the specific results in Figure 8.5. Are they what you would have expected?

* Explain the limitations of recursive binary splitting. This is a nonlinear method, so will it always outperform a linear method on nonlinear data? Why or why not?

* Imagine you are a consultant working on the baseball salary data from your text book. Would you rather show your clients Figure 8.1 or Figure 8.4? What motivates your choice, and how would you go about explaining your choice to your client?

 

Post your thoughts to the questions above and provide constructive feedback to at least two (2) of your peers.

You will in turn receive feedback from your colleagues and instructors, and have until EOD Sunday to address the feedback you receive from your colleagues and instructors before your discussion post will be graded.

assignments.description:
Unit 2 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser, hidden in app)

 

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The objective of this project is for students to preprocess acquired structured and unstructured datasets to transform raw data into a format suitable for machine learning algorithms. This includes feature extraction, removal of erroneous or redundant data, and outliers. Students will also practice exploratory data analysis and visualization techniques to examine patterns, relationships, and outliers in their data.

Key Focus Areas:

* Utilize key pandas functions like describe, info, and matplotlib to understand the data.

* Practice cleaning the data by replacing missing values, normalizing, and using PCA to transform the dataset.

* Once the dataset is prepared, use train_test_split to ready it for modeling.

* Additionally, work briefly with unstructured data to clean and prepare it for modeling.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Download and use this [Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063734?wrap=1)[ Download Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063903/download)for your solutions to the tasks below. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file, which you will submit.

Part 1: Structured Data
-----------------------

TASK: Complete the following steps (1 - 8) to work with data from a CSV file and perform a series of preprocessing tasks. These tasks will help you solidify your understanding by generating basic and advanced descriptive statistics, conducting exploratory data analysis, handling missing values and outliers, and practicing data scaling and reduction techniques.

Note: For each step, we have provided recommended activities along with some optional ones to practice preprocessing. If you discover additional or alternative experiments, feel free to include them in your analysis.

Steps 1 and 2
-------------

Step 1: Setup
-------------

* Load Libraries and Data: Import the necessary libraries into your Python environment

* Load the [flights-small.csv] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063924?wrap=1) dataset using Pandas into a dataframe

* Print the dataframe's first few elements using the .head() command to see what data you have

Step 2: Descriptive Statistics
------------------------------

Generate Basic Descriptive Statistics:

* Use Pandas to display basic information about the dataset, such as data types, number of non-null values, and memory usage.

* Generate summary statistics including mean, median, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum values for each numerical column.

Advanced Descriptive Statistics (optional):

* Calculate additional statistics such as skewness and kurtosis to understand the distribution and shape of the data using the following df functions:

df.skew()
df.kurtosis()

Note, you will likely have to convert some columns to numerical OR limit columns to be used using something like the below sample code:

df.select_dtypes(include=np.number)
OR
numerical_cols = ["col1", "col2", ...]
df[numerical_cols].skew()
df[numerical_cols].kurtosis()

* More optional extras:

* Generate a correlation matrix to identify relationships between numerical features. Visualize the matrix using a heatmap.

* To generate a correlation matrix, you can use df.corr(). You may need to use the same code as above to limit which columns are used.

Steps 3 and 4
-------------

Step 3: Exploratory Data Visualization
--------------------------------------

Visualize Distributions:

Create box plots to identify the presence of outliers in numerical features.

Visualize Relationships:

* Use scatter plots to visualize the relationship between pairs of numerical features.

* Use heatmaps to visualize the correlation matrix, showing the strength of relationships between features.

Visualize Categorical Features:

* Use count plots to visualize the frequency of categories in categorical features.

* Create bar plots to compare the mean or sum of numerical features across different categories.

Step 4: Data Cleansing
----------------------

Data Selection:

For this step, you will need to split the columns into numerical and non-numerical to apply the transformations appropriately, to do this use the following lists, then when applying transformations use the appropriate list:

numerical_columns = [
    'year', 'month', 'day', 'dep_time', 'sched_dep_time', 
    'dep_delay', 'arr_time', 'sched_arr_time', 'arr_delay', 
  'flight', 'air_time', 'distance', 'hour', 'minute' ]

non_numerical_columns = [
  'carrier', 'tailnum', 'origin', 'dest', 'time_hour', 'name' ]

Handle Missing Values:

* Identify columns with missing values using Pandas.

* Handle missing values by either filling them with appropriate statistics (mean, median, mode) or by removing rows/columns with missing values.

* Handle missing categorical values by filling with the mode

.fillna(df[column].mode()[0])

Handle Outliers:

* Identify outliers using visualizations like box plots.

* Remove outliers using statistical methods like the IQR (Interquartile Range) method or use more advanced techniques like Isolation Forest from Scikit-learn for outlier detection.

Steps 5 and 6
-------------

Step 5: Data Transformation
---------------------------

Normalize or Standardize Numerical Features:

Normalize or standardize numerical features to bring them onto a common scale using Scikit-learn's StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler.

Encode Categorical Features:

* Convert categorical features into numerical values using encoding techniques.

* Use one-hot encoding for nominal categorical features with no ordinal relationship.

* Use label encoding for ordinal categorical features.

Apply Custom Transformations:

Apply custom transformations to features if required, such as log transformations or polynomial feature generation.

Step 6: Dimensionality Reduction
--------------------------------

Apply PCA to Reduce Dimensionality:

* Use Principal Component Analysis (PCA) from Scikit-learn to reduce the number of features while retaining most of the variance in the data.

* Determine the number of principal components to retain by examining the explained variance ratio.

Advanced Dimensionality Reduction Techniques (optional):

* Explore other dimensionality reduction techniques like Kernel PCA, t-SNE, or LDA if the data has complex relationships or non-linear patterns.

Steps 7 and 8
-------------

Step 7: Data Balancing, Merging, Aggregation
--------------------------------------------

Aggregate Data:

* Aggregate data to summarize it by certain features using group-by operations in Pandas.

* Calculate aggregate statistics like mean, sum, count, etc., to generate summarized views of the data.

Step 8: Data Splitting (Train, Test, Validate)
----------------------------------------------

Split Data into Training, Testing, and Validation Sets:

* Split the dataset into training, testing, and validation sets using Scikit-learn's train_test_split function.

* Typically, allocate 70-80% of the data for training, 20-30% for testing, and optionally, 10-20% for validation.

* Ensure that the splits are representative of the overall dataset distribution.

Part 2: Unstructured Data
-------------------------

TASK: In this task, you will clean and preprocess the text data using several steps. You will start by removing punctuation, converting text to lowercase, tokenizing the data, and removing stop words. After preprocessing (using the provided sample code), you will apply TF-IDF to the cleaned data and display the results.

* Use the provided code and sample data as a starting place

* Run the code and print out the reviews list to ensure you are ready to clean and preprocess the text data

* Remove Punctuation: Use the nltk package to remove punctuation from the text data.

* Convert to Lowercase: Convert all text to lowercase using the lower() function.

* Tokenize the Data: Tokenize the text data to break it down into individual words or tokens.

* Remove Stop Words: Remove any stop words from the text to focus on the meaningful words.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file. To submit your notebook, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time, but for larger data sets in the future, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

assignments.description:
[SYGN 502_What is Good Assignment_FA25.pdf] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/75170/files/8270432?wrap=1)

 

What is Good? Assignment

 

DUE: On Canvas by class time (12pm) on October 6th

 

Points Value: 150 – 15% of overall grade

Minimum 500 words

 

Assignment: Conducting ethical research is important. But what constitutes ethical research and what is required to carry out this work can be different for different fields. With this assignment you are asked to explore what ethical research in your area of focus includes. This will mean seeking information about what forms of guidance are available for your work such as codes of ethics, regulatory requirements, and relevant case examples or other educational resources.

 

Additionally, in order to conduct ethical research, you must be able to do research. This often includes submitting research proposals, applying for grant funding, and getting institutional approval. Often, there are certain field-specific aspects of the research development process that include relevant ethical considerations such as needing IRB approval for any human subjects research, or needing to indicate data handling processes for research grant proposals.

 

For this assignment you will conduct a meaningful investigation of what Responsible Conduct of Research means in your field or area of specific research or professional destination.

* You will then submit a short summary of what you discover including references to sources which provide this guidance.

* You should reflect on how you will integrate these practices into your current and ongoing research and

* Include some form of personalized action plan or statement of intent.

 

You will be scored on the quality of your submission and its indication of the rigor of your work.

 

General guidelines:

 C (70 points) ~ Something turned in but it does not meet the requirements of the prompt

* B (80 points) ~ Something turned in which does meet 500 word requirement and requirements of the prompt but shows minimal input (is essentially a simple reflection piece)

* A (90 points) ~ Meets all requirements of the prompt and shows substantial research and integration of reflections and meaningful personalized action planning.

* Full 100 points ~ Meets all of the above AND goes beyond with additional materials or demonstrated action taken (e.g., completing ethical steps of a research proposal or IRB-type submission)

OBJECTIVE

The objective of this project is to introduce students to the concepts of Linear Regression and provide hands-on experience in implementing it using Python's Scikit-learn library. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file, which you will submit.

The Dataset
-----------

Dataset Selection: You will be using the Bike Sharing dataset, which includes various attributes related to bike rental counts in a city. This dataset is unique and provides a real-world application for predicting the number of bike rentals. You can access the data from the below URL or download this file ([day.csv] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063930?wrap=1)) and adjust the starter code provided in the notebook file.

url = "https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/00275/Bike-Sharing-Dataset.zip"

Dataset Description:

* The Bike Sharing dataset contains (one with 17,379 instances and one with 731 instances and approx. 16 features), such as date, temperature, humidity, wind speed, and more.

* The target variable is the count of total rentals on each day.

Data Preprocessing Suggestions
------------------------------

Loading Data: Load the Bike Sharing dataset using Pandas.

Data Exploration and Visualization: Produce some visualizations, statistics, etc. to gain an understanding for the dataset and what it contains.

Handling Missing Values: Check for and handle any missing values in the dataset (if any).

Standardization: Standardize the features to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 for consistent training. This step helps ensure that the model is not biased towards features with larger scales.

Train-Test Split: Split the dataset into training and testing sets to evaluate the model's performance on unseen data.

Building the Model
------------------

Model Initialization:

* Choosing Hyperparameters: Explain key hyperparameters such as the fit_intercept and n_jobs.

* Linear Regression Initialization: Use the LinearRegression class from Scikit-learn, specifying the chosen hyperparameters.

Model Fitting: Fit the Linear Regression model to the standardized Bike Sharing dataset.

Evaluating the Model
--------------------

Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and R-squared (R2) to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The objective of this project is to introduce students to the concepts of Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) and provide hands-on experience in implementing them using Python's Scikit-learn library. Students will learn how to preprocess data, build, fit, and evaluate a GMM, and understand the significance of various parameters and their impact on the model's performance.

NOTE: Even though Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) is an unsupervised learning method, when applied to anomaly detection, it can be treated similarly to a supervised task. After training the GMM on normal data, we can use a train-test split to evaluate its performance on new data. The model assigns probabilities to each point, and by setting a threshold on these probabilities, we can classify points as either normal or anomalous. This allows us to evaluate the model using traditional classification metrics like precision, recall, and the F1-score, just as we would in a supervised classification task.

The Dataset
-----------

Dataset Selection: You will be using the Iris dataset, which is a well-known dataset in the machine learning community. It includes various features of iris flowers and their species, making it suitable for clustering. You can access the data from the Scikit-learn's dataset library.

iris = load_iris()

Dataset Description:

* The Iris dataset contains 150 samples with four features each: sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width.

* The dataset is labeled with three classes: Iris-setosa, Iris-versicolor, and Iris-virginica.

Data Preprocessing
------------------

Loading Data: Load the Iris dataset using Scikit-learn’s load_iris function.

Standardization: Standardize the features to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 for consistent training. This step is crucial because K-Means uses Euclidean distance, which can be affected by different scales of features.

Building the Model
------------------

Model Initialization:

* Choosing K: Decide on the number of clusters (K). For the Iris dataset, K=3 is typically used because there are three known classes. Try it out with a few different values for k, do some score better than others?

* K-Means Initialization: Use the K-Means algorithm from Scikit-learn, specifying the number of clusters and other parameters such as the random state for reproducibility.

Model Fitting: Fit the K-Means model to the standardized Iris dataset. This process involves the iterative steps of the K-Means algorithm (assignment and update steps) until convergence.

Evaluating the Model
--------------------

Cluster Assignment: After fitting the model, obtain the cluster assignments for each data point.

Evaluation Metrics:

* Inertia: Calculate the within-cluster sum of squares (inertia) to measure how tightly the clusters are packed.

* Silhouette Score: Use the silhouette score to evaluate the quality of the clusters. This score measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters.

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The goal of this project is to introduce students to the concepts of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and provide hands-on experience in implementing them using TensorFlow v2. Students will learn to preprocess data, build, fit, and evaluate a CNN model, and understand the significance of various parameters and their impact on the model's performance. This project will involve using a unique dataset to classify images of rock, paper, and scissors.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Download and use this [Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063813?wrap=1) for your solutions to the tasks below. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file, which you will submit.

The Dataset
-----------

Dataset Selection: You will be using the Rock-Paper-Scissors image dataset, which includes images of hands showing rock, paper, and scissors gestures. This dataset is unique and provides a fun and real-world application for image classification using CNNs. You can access this dataset from TensorFlow's dataset library.

(train_data, val_data, test_data), info = tfds.load(
    'rock_paper_scissors',
    split=['train[:80%]', 'train[80%:90%]', 'train[90%:]'],
    with_info=True,
    as_supervised=True,
)

Dataset Description:

* The Rock-Paper-Scissors dataset contains 2,892 images divided into three classes: rock, paper, and scissors. Each class has around 1,000 images.

* The images are RGB with a size of 300x300 pixels.

Data Preprocessing
------------------

Loading Data: Load the Rock-Paper-Scissors dataset using TensorFlow Datasets.

Data Exploration and Visualization: Produce some visualizations, statistics, etc. to gain an understanding for the dataset and what it contains.

Data Augmentation: Apply data augmentation techniques such as rotation, zoom, and flipping to increase the diversity of the training data.

# Define data augmentation function
def augment(image, label):
    image = tf.image.resize(image, [150, 150])
    image = tf.image.random_flip_left_right(image)
    image = tf.image.random_flip_up_down(image)
    image = tf.image.random_brightness(image, max_delta=0.3)
    image = tf.image.random_contrast(image, lower=0.7, upper=1.3)
    return image, label

Standardization: Normalize the pixel values to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 for consistent training.

Train-Test Split: Split the dataset into training, validation, and testing sets to evaluate the model's performance on unseen data.

Building the Model
------------------

Model Initialization:

* Choosing Hyperparameters: Explain key hyperparameters such as the number of convolutional layers, filter sizes, kernel sizes, activation functions, and dropout rates.

* CNN Initialization: Use the TensorFlow and Keras libraries to define the CNN architecture.

Model Fitting: Fit the CNN model to the preprocessed Rock-Paper-Scissors dataset. Fit for ~10-20 epochs (you can do more but this may take extra time, remember, the training may take some time for such a complex model).

Evaluating the Model
--------------------

Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file. To submit your notebook, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time, but for larger data sets in the future, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

Unit 3 Part 3 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser, hidden in app)

 

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The objective of this project is to introduce students to the concepts of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) and provide hands-on experience in implementing them using TensorFlow. Students will learn how to preprocess data, build, train, and evaluate an ANN model on two different types of data (e.g., image and numerical), and understand the significance of various hyperparameters and their impact on the model's performance.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Download and use this [Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063915?wrap=1) for your solutions to the tasks below. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file, which you will submit.

The Dataset(s)
--------------

Dataset Selection: You will be using two different types of data (e.g., image and numerical). Although the datasets and preprocessing tasks differ, you will be performing the same steps for building and evaluating your models. You can access both of these datasets from the Scikit-learn's dataset library.

* Part 1: CIFAR-10 Dataset - Image Based

(X_train, y_train), (X_test, y_test) = cifar10.load_data()

* Part 2: California Housing Dataset - Numerical Based

data = fetch_california_housing()

Dataset Descriptions:

* Part 1: CIFAR-10 Dataset - Image Based

The CIFAR-10 dataset is a widely used benchmark dataset in machine learning and computer vision. It consists of 60,000 color images in 10 different classes, with 6,000 images per class. The dataset is divided into 50,000 training images and 10,000 test images.

* Part 2: California Housing Dataset - Numerical Based

The California Housing dataset is a classic dataset used for regression tasks. It consists of information collected from the 1990 California census, which aims to predict the median house value in various districts based on several features.

Data Preprocessing
------------------

* Part 1: CIFAR-10 Dataset - Image Based

Loading Data: Load the Iris dataset using Scikit-learn’s cifar10.load_data() function.

Normalization: Normalize the pixel values of images to a range of 0 to 1 to ensure uniformity and faster convergence during training.

One-Hot Encoding: Convert categorical labels into binary class matrices (one-hot encoding) to be used with neural networks.

* Part 2: California Housing Dataset - Numerical Based

Loading Data: Load the Iris dataset using Scikit-learn’s fetch_california_housing() function.

Handling Missing Values: Identify and handle any missing values in the dataset (e.g., using mean or median imputation).

Feature Scaling: Standardize the features to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 for consistent training.

Train-Test Split: Split the dataset into training and testing sets to evaluate the model's performance on unseen data.

Building the Model
------------------

Model Architecture:

* Input Layer: Define the input shape based on the dataset (e.g., 32x32x3 for CIFAR-10, number of features for California Housing Prices).

* Hidden Layers: Add several hidden layers with a specified number of neurons and activation functions (e.g., ReLU). Experiment with different architectures.

* Output Layer: Define the output layer with an appropriate activation function (e.g., softmax for classification, linear for regression).

Compilation:

* Loss Function: Choose a suitable loss function based on the task (e.g., categorical crossentropy for classification, mean squared error for regression).

* Optimizer: Select an optimizer like Adam or SGD to minimize the loss function during training.

* Metrics: Define evaluation metrics to monitor the training process (e.g., accuracy for classification, mean absolute error for regression).

Model Training:

* Epochs: Set the number of epochs, which determine how many times the model will iterate over the entire training dataset.

* Batch Size: Choose a batch size that specifies the number of samples processed before updating the model’s parameters.

* Validation Split: Use a portion of the training data as a validation set to monitor the model's performance during training and avoid overfitting.

* Monitoring Training: Track the training and validation accuracy/loss to observe the model's learning progress and detect any signs of overfitting or underfitting.

Evaluating the Model
--------------------

Testing Data: Evaluate the model's performance on the testing dataset to assess its generalization capability.

Performance Metrics: Calculate relevant metrics to quantify the model's performance, such as accuracy, precision, and recall for classification tasks, and mean absolute error, mean squared error, and root mean squared error for regression tasks.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file. To submit your notebook, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time, but for larger data sets in the future, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

assignments.description:
Unit 4 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser, hidden in app)

 

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The primary objective of this project is to engage in deep-level thinking and apply the concepts we've learned during the results and evaluation stage of our workflow.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Select TWO (2) of your favorite projects, each representing a different type of model we studied during the model selection portion of the class (e.g., one clustering and one instance-based).

* Apply techniques for model evaluation and parameter tuning to these projects. In the next section, you will find recommendations for each model.

* For each model you selected, provide a brief explanation at the bottom of the Jupyter Notebook File (about a paragraph) detailing the techniques you used, their impact on the solution (whether it improved or worsened), the parameters you tuned, and your overall thoughts on the performance.

------------------
SELECT YOUR MODELS
------------------

For each model we have studied, we have provided recommended activities for evaluating your model and tuning its hyperparameters/parameters. If you discover additional or alternative experiments, feel free to include them in your analysis.

Linear Regression
-----------------

Linear Regression
-----------------

Evaluating the Model

* Model Evaluation:

* Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and R-squared (R2) to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Residual Analysis: Plot the residuals to understand the difference between the observed and predicted values.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Parameter Tuning:

* Polynomial Features and Cross-Validation: Instead of tuning fit_intercept and normalize parameters, we will use polynomial features to improve our Linear Regression model. We will systematically explore different polynomial degrees to identify the degree that results in the best performance. Additionally, we will employ cross-validation to ensure that the tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

* Specific Tuning Steps:

* Define polynomial degrees to try:

degrees = [1, 2, 3].

*

* For each degree:

* Use PolynomialFeatures from Scikit-learn to create polynomial features.

* Transform the training and testing data using the polynomial features.

* Initialize and fit a LinearRegression model.

* Perform cross-validation using cross_val_score to evaluate the model's performance.

* Identify the degree with the best cross-validation score.

* Refit the model with the best degree using the entire training set.

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

K-Nearest Neighbors
-------------------

K-Nearest Neighbors
-------------------

Evaluating the Model

Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Grid Search: Use grid search to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters for the KNN model and identify the combination that results in the best performance. Hyperparameters to tune include n_neighbors, metric, and weights. Specific Grid Search Steps:

* Define a parameter grid:

param_grid = {'n_neighbors': [3, 5, 7, 9], 'metric': ['euclidean', 'manhattan', 'minkowski'], 'weights': ['uniform', 'distance']}

*

* Use GridSearchCV from Scikit-learn to perform the grid search:

GridSearchCV(estimator=KNeighborsClassifier(), param_grid=param_grid, cv=5, scoring='accuracy', verbose=2, n_jobs=-1)

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

Support Vector Machines
-----------------------

Support Vector Machines
-----------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Model Evaluation: Calculate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Visualization (optional): Visualize the decision boundary (if applicable) to understand how the SVM separates the different classes.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Grid Search: Use grid search to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters for the SVM model and identify the combination that results in the best performance. Hyperparameters to tune include C, kernel, and gamma. Specific Grid Search Steps:

* Define a parameter grid:

param_grid = {'C': [0.1, 1, 10, 100], 'gamma': [1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001], 'kernel': ['linear', 'rbf', 'poly']}

*

* Use GridSearchCV from Scikit-learn to perform the grid search:

GridSearchCV(estimator=SVC(), param_grid=param_grid, cv=5, scoring='accuracy', verbose=2, n_jobs=-1)

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

Decision Trees
--------------

Decision Trees
--------------

Evaluating the Model

* Bagging Implementation:

* Bagging Initialization: Use the BaggingClassifier from Scikit-learn, wrapping the DecisionTreeClassifier. Specify the number of base estimators (n_estimators) and other relevant parameters.

* Model Fitting: Fit the Bagging model to the standardized Wine Quality dataset.

* Evaluation of Bagging Model: Evaluate the performance of the Bagging model using the same metrics as before and compare the results with the single decision tree model.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Model Evaluation: Calculate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Visualization (optional): Visualize the decision tree structure to understand how decisions are made based on different features.

Random Forests
--------------

Random Forests
--------------

Evaluating the Model

* Model Evaluation: Calculate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Feature Importance: Analyze the importance of each feature in making predictions by examining the feature importances attribute of the Random Forest model. You can access these by using the following:

#Get feature importances
importances = rf.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)[::-1]
feature_names = df.columns[:-1]

Tuning Model Parameters

Parameters:

#Number of trees in the forest
n_estimators = 100  

#Maximum depth of the tree
max_depth = None  

#Minimum number of samples required to split an internal node
min_samples_split = 2

#Number of features to consider when looking for the best split
max_features = 'sqrt'

* Grid Search: Use grid search to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters and identify the combination that results in the best performance. Hyperparameters to tune include n_estimators, max_depth, min_samples_split, and max_features.

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

k-Means Clustering
------------------

k-Means Clustering
------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Cluster Assignment: After fitting the model, obtain the cluster assignments for each data point.

* Evaluation Metrics:

* Inertia: Calculate the within-cluster sum of squares (inertia) to measure how tightly the clusters are packed.

* Silhouette Score: Use the silhouette score to evaluate the quality of the clusters. This score measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Choosing the Optimal K: Experiment with different numbers of clusters (K) and use methods like the Elbow Method and Silhouette Score to determine the optimal number of clusters.

* Elbow Method: Plot the inertia for different values of K and look for an “elbow” point where the inertia starts to decrease more slowly.

* Silhouette Score: Calculate the silhouette score for different values of K and choose the value that maximizes the score.

* Re-fitting the Model: After determining the optimal number of clusters, refit the K-Means model using the selected K.

Gaussian Mixture Model
----------------------

Gaussian Mixture Model
----------------------

Evaluating the Model

Real-World Data: Evaluate the anomaly detection performance using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and confusion matrix.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Parameter Tuning:

* Number of Components: Experiment with different numbers of Gaussian components to find the optimal value.

* Covariance Type: Test various covariance types and observe their impact on the model's performance.

* Techniques: Use methods like cross-validation to systematically explore the parameter space and identify the optimal settings.

Artificial Neural Networks
--------------------------

Artificial Neural Networks
--------------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Testing Data: Evaluate the model's performance on the testing dataset to assess its generalization capability.

* Performance Metrics: Calculate relevant metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall for classification; mean absolute error, mean squared error for regression) to quantify the model's performance.

Tuning Model Parameters

Let’s try a few different models, with Tensorflow you have the option to try different layers and all of their different parameters. Please do the following:

* Try at least 2 more model configurations

* For the configurations, pick two layers and try different parameters for them.

* Answer the following

* How do the new models compare to the others, what do you think causes the improvement or decay of the model performance?

* What parameters did you choose and why, what was their overall impact on model performance?

Convolutional Neural Networks
-----------------------------

Convolutional Neural Networks
-----------------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Loss and Accuracy Curves: Plot the loss and accuracy curves for the training and validation sets to understand the model’s learning process.

Tuning Model Parameters

Hyperparameter Tuning: Use techniques such as learning rate scheduling, early stopping, and model checkpointing to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters for the CNN model and identify the combination that results in the best performance.

Specific Tuning Steps:

*

Define a range of learning rates:

learning_rates = [0.001, 0.0001, 0.00001]

*

Implement early stopping to avoid overfitting:

EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', patience=5)

*

Use model checkpointing to save the best model:

ModelCheckpoint(filepath='best_model.h5', monitor='val_loss', save_best_only=True)

Download Sample Images for Predictions (optional):

[rock_sample] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063843/download)      [paper sample] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063704/download)      [scissors sample] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063819/download)

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file(s). To submit your notebook, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time, but for larger data sets in the future, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The overall objective of this project is to package your work by applying the concepts learned during the data storytelling and advanced visualization stages of our workflow.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Select TWO (2) of your favorite projects, each representing a different type of model we studied during the model selection portion of the class (e.g., one clustering and one instance-based). This can be new ones or the same projects selected in the Results and Evaluation Project.

* Apply data storytelling techniques, including advanced or explanatory visualizations, to these projects. In the next section, you will find recommendations for visualizations you can use for each model.

* For each model you selected, prepare a detailed report in the form of a Jupyter Notebook File (or pdf report) summarizing the project, including the following sections:

* Audience: Before starting you will need to choose who your presentation will be aimed at. Please provide a paragraph that details who the stakeholders are, what they may know or not know and how you will tailor your presentation to their needs. Some ideas are data scientist, students, managers, C-suite executives, general public, etc.

* Introduction: Provide a brief overview of the problem and the chosen dataset.

* Data Preprocessing: Describe the data preprocessing steps, including loading, data exploration and visualization, handling missing values, encoding categorical variables, feature engineering, and standardizing the data.

* Model Architecture: Explain the model and the selected parameters.

* Training and Evaluation: Summarize the training process, the evaluation metrics used, and the results obtained.

* Parameter Tuning: Discuss the experiments conducted to find the optimal hyperparameters and their outcomes.

* Visualizations (see below for recommendations for visualizations you can use for each model).

* Conclusion: Share insights gained from the project and potential improvements for future work.

------------------
SELECT YOUR MODELS
------------------

For each model we have studied, we have provided recommended visualizations you can use. If you discover additional or alternative ideas, feel free to include them in your analysis.

Linear Regression
-----------------

Linear Regression
-----------------

* Residual Plot: Visualize the residual plot to understand the model’s performance in detail, including how well the model captures the patterns in the data.

* Prediction Plot: Plot the predicted vs. actual values to visualize how well the model predicts the target variable.

* Learning Curves: Plot learning curves to show the model’s performance (e.g., MSE) as a function of the number of training samples. This helps in understanding how the model’s performance improves with more data.

K-Nearest Neighbors
-------------------

K-Nearest Neighbors
-------------------

* Confusion Matrix: Visualize the confusion matrix to understand the model’s performance in detail, including how many instances were correctly or incorrectly classified for each class.

* Learning Curves: Plot learning curves to show the model’s performance (e.g., accuracy) as a function of the number of training samples. This helps in understanding how the model’s performance improves with more data.

Support Vector Machines
-----------------------

Support Vector Machines
-----------------------

* Confusion Matrix: Visualize the confusion matrix to understand the model’s performance in detail, including how many instances were correctly or incorrectly classified for each class.

* Decision Boundary: Visualize the decision boundary (if applicable) to understand how the SVM separates the different classes.

* Learning Curves: Plot learning curves to show the model’s performance (e.g., accuracy) as a function of the number of training samples. This helps in understanding how the model’s performance improves with more data. Using sklearn or another library:
https://scikit-learn.org/stable/auto_examples/model_selection/plot_learning_curve.html 

Decision Trees
--------------

Decision Trees
--------------

* Confusion Matrix: Visualize the confusion matrix to understand the model’s performance in detail, including how many instances were correctly or incorrectly classified for each class.

* Feature Importance Plot: Create a bar plot of feature importances to visualize which features are most influential in the model’s predictions.

# Feature importance for the base decision tree

indices = np.argsort(importances)[::-1]
feature_names = df.columns[:-1]

* Learning Curves: Plot learning curves to show the model’s performance (e.g., accuracy) as a function of the number of training samples. This helps in understanding how the model’s performance improves with more data.

* Plot Best Tree: Using sklearn or another library, plot the decision tree.

* https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.tree.plot_tree.html 

Random Forests
--------------

Random Forests
--------------

* Confusion Matrix: Visualize the confusion matrix to understand the model’s performance in detail, including how many instances were correctly or incorrectly classified for each class.

* Feature Importance Plot: Create a bar plot of feature importances to visualize which features are most influential in the model’s predictions.

# Get feature importances

* Learning Curves: Plot learning curves to show the model’s performance (e.g., accuracy) as a function of the number of training samples. This helps in understanding how the model’s performance improves with more data.

k-Means Clustering
------------------

k-Means Clustering
------------------

* Scatter Plot: Create a scatter plot of the data points with colors indicating their cluster assignments. Highlight the cluster centroids on the plot.

* Pair Plot: For multi-dimensional data like the Iris dataset, use pair plots to visualize the clustering results across different feature combinations.

* Centroids: Plot the centroids of the clusters to show the center of each cluster.

* Sources: https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.cluster.KMeans.html 

Gaussian Mixture Model
----------------------

Gaussian Mixture Model
----------------------

Anomaly Detection Visualization:

* Real-World Data: Plot the distribution of anomaly scores and visualize the detected anomalies.

* Visualize the clusters and their distributions using scatter plots and contour plots.

Artificial Neural Networks
--------------------------

Artificial Neural Networks
--------------------------

* Accuracy/Loss Plots: Plot the training and validation accuracy/loss over epochs to visualize the model's learning process.

* Interpretation: Analyze the plots to understand the model's behavior (e.g., detecting overfitting, underfitting).

Convolutional Neural Networks
-----------------------------

Convolutional Neural Networks
-----------------------------

* Confusion Matrix: Visualize the confusion matrix to understand the model’s performance in detail, including how many instances were correctly or incorrectly classified for each class.

* Loss and Accuracy Curves: Plot the loss and accuracy curves to visualize the model’s training process and detect any signs of overfitting or underfitting.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file(s) or pdf(s). To submit your notebook, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time, but for larger data sets in the future, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

assignments.title: Presentation of a Research Paper: Understanding and Critique State of the Art Research of Machine Learning
assignments.description:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Presentation of a Research Paper: Understanding and Critique State of the Art Research of Machine Learning
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Overview
--------

Machine learning is a powerful\, yet new\, computational toolset in computer science. Because it is a fast-evolving scientific area\, state-of-the-art machine learning methods and new achievements are usually published in recent academic conferences\, instead of any textbooks. Therefore\, it is of paramount importance to read papers to have a better understanding on machine learning theories and algorithms. To this end\, every student in this class is expected to read at least one research paper published in top machine learning conferences when taking this course in this semester. Papers can be selected from the proceedings of the following two best machine learning conferences held in the past 5 years (2017-2021/2022):

* International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML): [ICML 2024] (https://icml.cc/virtual/2024/papers.html?filter=titles)\, [2023] (https://icml.cc/Conferences/2023)\, [2022] (https://proceedings.mlr.press/v162/) [2021] (https://proceedings.mlr.press/v139/)\, [2020 (Links to an external site.)] (http://proceedings.mlr.press/v119/)\, [2019 (Links to an external site.)] (http://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/)\, [2018 (Links to an external site.)] (http://proceedings.mlr.press/v80/)[ (Links to an external site.)] (http://proceedings.mlr.press/v70/)[ (Links to an external site.)] (http://proceedings.mlr.press/v48/)

* [Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)] (https://papers.nips.cc/)

Part One (70 points) - Video Presentation (due in Week 6)
---------------------------------------------------------

Every student is required to select one paper from the above proceedings and present it by a video posted on this discussion board. In your post\, please make sure that the following information/items are clearly provided:

*

The title of the paper which you select for presentation and the proceedings in which the paper was published.

* It would be great if you can link your presented paper to where it is published. If you do not know how to use the external link in your discussion post\, please refer to [the post here (Links to an external site.)] (https://community.canvaslms.com/t5/Instructor-Guide/How-do-I-create-hyperlinks-to-external-URLs-in-the-Rich-Content/ta-p/872) or contact the instructor for help.

* Your presentation video.

* While we have no requirement on the length of your presentation\, a reasonable presentation in most academic conferences takes 10-20 minutes. Thus\, you are suggested to keep the length of your presentation video in this range.

* In case you do not know how to record your presentation video\, please take a look at [a brief instruction posted here] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76715/files/8388580?wrap=1) for recording by zoom or [a tutorial posted here] (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=83L8BleVBVo&t=194s) for recording by Microsoft PowerPoint.

* A list of three questions about your presentation for your peer(s) in the class to answer or discuss.

Presentations must be posted by 11:59 PM on the Friday of Week 6 for full credit.

* Presentation slides (20 points): slides are expected to be well structured\, easy to understand\, sufficiently detailed (but not cluttered)\, comprehensive with complete contents of the selected paper. Designing the slides using your own understanding and language is HIGHLY encouraged. Please avoid:

* slides filled with only texts\;

* slides made just by copy-paste from the original paper.

* Presentations (50 points): clear and active presentations are expected. The following are suggested questions to answer in your presentation (you do not have to answer all the questions below):

* What is the motivation for the work in the paper (both people problem and technical problem)\, and its distillation into a research question? Why doesn't the people problem have a trivial solution? What are the previous solutions and why are they inadequate?

* What is the proposed solution (hypothesis\, idea\, design)? Why is it believed it will work? How does it represent an improvement? How is the solution achieved?

* What is the author's evaluation of the solution? What logic\, argument\, evidence\, artifacts (e.g.\, a proof-of-concept system)\, or experiments are presented in support of the idea?

* What is your analysis of the identified problem\, idea\, and evaluation? Is this a good idea? What flaws do you perceive in the work? What are the most interesting or controversial ideas? For work that has practical implications\, ask whether this will work\, who would want it\, what it will take to give it to them\, and when might it become a reality?

* What are the paper's contributions (author's and your opinion)? Ideas\, methods\, software\, experimental results\, experimental techniques...?

* What are future directions for this research (author's and yours\, perhaps driven by shortcomings or other critiques)?

* What questions are you left with? What questions would you like to raise in an open discussion of the work (review interesting and controversial points\, above)? What do you find difficult to understand?

Part Two (20 points) - Peer Reviews of Presentations (due in Week 7)
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Please every student watch the presentation videos posted in this discussion assignment. Every student is required to participate the discussions at least 3 times by\, for example\, answering the questions raised by the presenting student of a presentation\, asking questions for clarification or new discussion topics\, or commenting on discussions by other students. The discussion response may include\, but not limited to\, extending the thinking on the papers\, pointing out flaws of paper or the presentation\, to name a few.

Participation responses must be posted by 11:59 PM on the Friday of Week 7 for full credit.

Part Three (10 points) - Presenters Respond to Peer Feedback (due in Week 8)
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Every student is required to answer or comment to the responses posted to the presentation video by other students\, if any. The presenting students are expected to accurately and completely answer the questions raised to your paper presentation or your questions by responding\, for example\, the following points: explain your takeaways from the peer the feedback\, such as: is it valid\, did they help find a gap that might have been problematic\, to name a few.

Responses by the presenting students must be posted by 11:59 PM on Wednesday Week 8 for full credit.

Grading
-------

View the [discussion rubric] (https://community.canvaslms.com/t5/Student-Guide/How-do-I-view-the-rubric-for-my-graded-discussion/ta-p/319) for grading details.

assignments.description:
Unit 3 Part 2 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser\, hidden in app)

 

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The goal of this project is to introduce students to the concepts of Decision Trees and provide hands-on experience in implementing Decision Trees with Bagging using Python's Scikit-learn library. Students will learn to preprocess data\, build\, fit\, and evaluate a Decision Tree model\, and understand the significance of various parameters and their impact on the model's performance. Additionally\, this project will cover the concept of bagging to enhance the robustness and accuracy of Decision Trees. Students will work with a wine dataset to predict its quality based on certain features.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Download and use this [Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063747?wrap=1) for your solutions to the tasks below. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file\, which you will submit.

The Dataset
-----------

Dataset Selection: You will be using the Wine Quality dataset\, which contains various chemical properties of wines along with their quality ratings. Dataset Selection: You can access the data from the below URL

url = "https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/wine-quality/winequality-red.csv"

Dataset Description:

* The Wine Quality dataset contains samples with features representing various chemical properties (e.g.\, acidity\, residual sugar\, alcohol content).

* The target variable is a quality rating on a scale from 0 to 10.

Data Preprocessing
------------------

Loading Data: Load the Wine Quality dataset using Pandas.

Data Exploration and Visualization: Produce some visualizations\, statistics\, etc. to gain an understanding for the dataset and what it contains.

Handling Missing Values: Check for and handle any missing values in the dataset.

Standardization: Standardize the features to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 for consistent training. This step helps ensure that the model is not biased towards features with larger scales.

Target: The target in the dataset is “quality”.

Train-Test Split: Split the dataset into training and testing sets to evaluate the model's performance on unseen data.

Building the Model
------------------

Model Initialization:

* Choosing Hyperparameters: Explain key hyperparameters such as the maximum depth of the tree (max_depth)\, minimum samples per split (min_samples_split)\, and the criterion used for splitting (criterion).

* Decision Tree Initialization: Use the DecisionTreeClassifier from Scikit-learn\, specifying the chosen hyperparameters.

Model Fitting: Fit the Decision Tree model to the standardized Wine Quality dataset.

Evaluating the Model
--------------------

Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as accuracy\, precision\, recall\, F1 score\, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file. To submit your notebook\, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time\, but for larger data sets in the future\, it will make the file smaller). ---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The objective of this project is to introduce students to the concepts of Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) and provide hands-on experience in implementing them using Python's Scikit-learn library. Students will learn how to preprocess data\, build\, fit\, and evaluate a GMM\, and understand the significance of various parameters and their impact on the model's performance.

NOTE: Even though Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) is an unsupervised learning method\, when applied to anomaly detection\, it can be treated similarly to a supervised task. After training the GMM on normal data\, we can use a train-test split to evaluate its performance on new data. The model assigns probabilities to each point\, and by setting a threshold on these probabilities\, we can classify points as either normal or anomalous. This allows us to evaluate the model using traditional classification metrics like precision\, recall\, and the F1-score\, just as we would in a supervised classification task.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Download and use this [Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063820?wrap=1) for your solutions to the tasks below. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file\, which you will submit.

Building the Model

Model Fitting: Fit the GMM to the features of the real-world dataset for anomaly detection.

Evaluating the Model

Dataset Selection: You will be using the Iris dataset\, which is a well-known dataset in the machine learning community. It includes various features of iris flowers and their species\, making it suitable for clustering. You can access the data from the Scikit-learn's dataset library.

iris = load_iris()

Dataset Description:

* The Iris dataset contains 150 samples with four features each: sepal length\, sepal width\, petal length\, and petal width.

* The dataset is labeled with three classes: Iris-setosa\, Iris-versicolor\, and Iris-virginica.

Data Preprocessing
------------------

Loading Data: Load the Iris dataset using Scikit-learn’s load_iris function.

Standardization: Standardize the features to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 for consistent training. This step is crucial because K-Means uses Euclidean distance\, which can be affected by different scales of features.

Building the Model
------------------

Model Initialization:

* Choosing K: Decide on the number of clusters (K). For the Iris dataset\, K=3 is typically used because there are three known classes. Try it out with a few different values for k\, do some score better than others?

* K-Means Initialization: Use the K-Means algorithm from Scikit-learn\, specifying the number of clusters and other parameters such as the random state for reproducibility.

Model Fitting: Fit the K-Means model to the standardized Iris dataset. This process involves the iterative steps of the K-Means algorithm (assignment and update steps) until convergence.

Evaluating the Model
--------------------

Cluster Assignment: After fitting the model\, obtain the cluster assignments for each data point.

Evaluation Metrics:

* Inertia: Calculate the within-cluster sum of squares (inertia) to measure how tightly the clusters are packed.

* Silhouette Score: Use the silhouette score to evaluate the quality of the clusters. This score measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file. To submit your notebook\, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time\, but for larger data sets in the future\, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally\, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

assignments.title: Project: Model Selection - Artificial Neural Networks
assignments.description:
Unit 3 Part 3 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser\, hidden in app)

 

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The objective of this project is to introduce students to the concepts of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) and provide hands-on experience in implementing them using TensorFlow. Students will learn how to preprocess data\, build\, train\, and evaluate an ANN model on two different types of data (e.g.\, image and numerical)\, and understand the significance of various hyperparameters and their impact on the model's performance.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Download and use this [Jupyter Notebook Starter File] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063915?wrap=1) for your solutions to the tasks below. Please complete all of your work for this project in the same Jupyter Notebook file\, which you will submit.

The Dataset(s)
--------------

Dataset Selection: You will be using two different types of data (e.g.\, image and numerical). Although the datasets and preprocessing tasks differ\, you will be performing the same steps for building and evaluating your models. You can access both of these datasets from the Scikit-learn's dataset library.

* Part 1: CIFAR-10 Dataset - Image Based

(X_train\, y_train)\, (X_test\, y_test) = cifar10.load_data()

* Part 2: California Housing Dataset - Numerical Based

data = fetch_california_housing()

Dataset Descriptions:

* Part 1: CIFAR-10 Dataset - Image Based

The CIFAR-10 dataset is a widely used benchmark dataset in machine learning and computer vision. It consists of 60\,000 color images in 10 different classes\, with 6\,000 images per class. The dataset is divided into 50\,000 training images and 10\,000 test images.

* Part 2: California Housing Dataset - Numerical Based

The California Housing dataset is a classic dataset used for regression tasks. It consists of information collected from the 1990 California census\, which aims to predict the median house value in various districts based on several features.

Data Preprocessing
------------------

* Part 1: CIFAR-10 Dataset - Image Based

Loading Data: Load the Iris dataset using Scikit-learn’s cifar10.load_data() function.

Normalization: Normalize the pixel values of images to a range of 0 to 1 to ensure uniformity and faster convergence during training.

One-Hot Encoding: Convert categorical labels into binary class matrices (one-hot encoding) to be used with neural networks.

* Part 2: California Housing Dataset - Numerical Based

Loading Data: Load the Iris dataset using Scikit-learn’s fetch_california_housing() function.

Handling Missing Values: Identify and handle any missing values in the dataset (e.g.\, using mean or median imputation).

Feature Scaling: Standardize the features to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1 for consistent training.

Train-Test Split: Split the dataset into training and testing sets to evaluate the model's performance on unseen data.

Building the Model
------------------

Model Architecture:

* Input Layer: Define the input shape based on the dataset (e.g.\, 32x32x3 for CIFAR-10\, number of features for California Housing Prices).

* Hidden Layers: Add several hidden layers with a specified number of neurons and activation functions (e.g.\, ReLU). Experiment with different architectures.

* Output Layer: Define the output layer with an appropriate activation function (e.g.\, softmax for classification\, linear for regression).

Compilation:

* Loss Function: Choose a suitable loss function based on the task (e.g.\, categorical crossentropy for classification\, mean squared error for regression).

* Optimizer: Select an optimizer like Adam or SGD to minimize the loss function during training.

* Metrics: Define evaluation metrics to monitor the training process (e.g.\, accuracy for classification\, mean absolute error for regression).

Model Training:

* Epochs: Set the number of epochs\, which determine how many times the model will iterate over the entire training dataset.

* Batch Size: Choose a batch size that specifies the number of samples processed before updating the model’s parameters.

* Validation Split: Use a portion of the training data as a validation set to monitor the model's performance during training and avoid overfitting.

* Monitoring Training: Track the training and validation accuracy/loss to observe the model's learning progress and detect any signs of overfitting or underfitting.

Evaluating the Model
--------------------

Testing Data: Evaluate the model's performance on the testing dataset to assess its generalization capability.

Performance Metrics: Calculate relevant metrics to quantify the model's performance\, such as accuracy\, precision\, and recall for classification tasks\, and mean absolute error\, mean squared error\, and root mean squared error for regression tasks.

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file. To submit your notebook\, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time\, but for larger data sets in the future\, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally\, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.
