assignments.title: Project Proposal: Prepare Scientific and Technical Report
assignments.description:
----------------------------------------------------------
 Project Proposal: Prepare Scientific and Technical Report
----------------------------------------------------------

Instructions
------------

Machine learning is a useful computational tool to deal with data problems that arise in our daily lives\, industry\, and scientific research. To demonstrate your understanding of the machine learning algorithms covered in this course\, you are required to propose one programming task to use any machine learning algorithm to solve a real-world problem. You are highly recommended to propose a project that is closely related to your own research. In the following\, three example projects are provided for your reference. You are also encouraged to use the materials on Internet for reference\, but you are required to write your own codes to implement your selected algorithm.

Student Samples
---------------

* Task 1: Bayesian learning for classifying Netnews text articles. Naive Bayes classifiers are among the most successful known algorithms for learning to classify text documents. We provide a dataset containing 20\,000 newsgroup messages drawn from the 20 newsgroups. The dataset contains 1000 documents from each of the 20 newsgroups. Please download the [zip file] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76715/files/8388456?wrap=1) for the data[Download at this link] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76715/files/8388456/download). Then you are required to process the articles (such as using bag-of-word model to represent the articles) and write a Naive Bayes' classifier to classify the news articles into 20 classes. You are required to perform the following subtasks.

*

* Perform class-vs-class classification\, i.e.\, pairwise binary classification. Please compare the performance of the classifications for different pair of classes\, and analyze why some are good but some are not.

* Perform tri-class classification (i.e. three class are involved). You can pick up at least five groups of tri-classes of data. Please compare the performance of the classifications for different group of classes\, and analyze why some are good but some are not.

* Task 2: Support vector machines for face recognition. Face recognition is a learning problem that has recently received a lot of attention. One standard approach involves reducing the dimensionality of the problem using PCA and then selecting the nearest class (eigenfaces). Support Vector Machines (SVM) are becoming very popular in the machine learning community as a technique for tackling high-dimensional problems. Can SVMs outperform standard face recognition algorithms? Please implement the SVM algorithm by yourself. Down the [experimental dataset] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76715/files/8388420?wrap=1) if this interests you.

* Task 3: Neural network learning to recognize faces. A neural network learning algorithm called Back-propagation is among the most effective approaches to machine learning when the data includes complex sensory input such as images. You can download the [data set ] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76715/files/8388460?wrap=1)with over 600 face images[Download here] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76715/files/8388460/download). The data set contains face images from 20 different person with different emotion and poses. Write your codes to implement the neural network\, by which you are expected to classify the emotions. That is\, you are expected to classify the images into four classes: neutral\, happy\, sad\, angry. Then compare the results between the following two cases: (1) include the face images with sunglasses in training and (2) not include face images with sunglasses in training\, and analyze your results.

Deliverables and Submissions
----------------------------

The project proposal is where you declare your plan to complete this assignment. For your selected project\, the proposal should not exceed 1 page and cover the following:

* Problem statement - identifying the problem and motivation to solve it

* Solution - solution description\, what area of machine learning and what machine learning algorithm the solution will utilize\, why this can't be done with traditional methods\, and input/output expectation

* Data - what data you have found related to this problem\, if any\, or your approach to acquiring data

* Timeline - The expected tasks to complete and weekly progress expectation

Please refer to the rubric for grading details.

assignments.description:
Unit 4 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser\, hidden in app)

 

---------
OBJECTIVE
---------

The primary objective of this project is to engage in deep-level thinking and apply the concepts we've learned during the results and evaluation stage of our workflow.

------------
INSTRUCTIONS
------------

Select TWO (2) of your favorite projects\, each representing a different type of model we studied during the model selection portion of the class (e.g.\, one clustering and one instance-based).

* Apply techniques for model evaluation and parameter tuning to these projects. In the next section\, you will find recommendations for each model.

* For each model you selected\, provide a brief explanation at the bottom of the Jupyter Notebook File (about a paragraph) detailing the techniques you used\, their impact on the solution (whether it improved or worsened)\, the parameters you tuned\, and your overall thoughts on the performance.

------------------
SELECT YOUR MODELS
------------------

For each model we have studied\, we have provided recommended activities for evaluating your model and tuning its hyperparameters/parameters. If you discover additional or alternative experiments\, feel free to include them in your analysis.

Linear Regression
-----------------

Linear Regression
-----------------

Evaluating the Model

* Model Evaluation:

* Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE)\, Mean Squared Error (MSE)\, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)\, and R-squared (R2) to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Residual Analysis: Plot the residuals to understand the difference between the observed and predicted values.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Parameter Tuning:

* Polynomial Features and Cross-Validation: Instead of tuning fit_intercept and normalize parameters\, we will use polynomial features to improve our Linear Regression model. We will systematically explore different polynomial degrees to identify the degree that results in the best performance. Additionally\, we will employ cross-validation to ensure that the tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

* Specific Tuning Steps:

* Define polynomial degrees to try:

degrees = [1\, 2\, 3].

*

* For each degree:

* Use PolynomialFeatures from Scikit-learn to create polynomial features.

* Transform the training and testing data using the polynomial features.

* Initialize and fit a LinearRegression model.

* Perform cross-validation using cross_val_score to evaluate the model's performance.

* Identify the degree with the best cross-validation score.

* Refit the model with the best degree using the entire training set.

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

K-Nearest Neighbors
-------------------

K-Nearest Neighbors
-------------------

Evaluating the Model

Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as accuracy\, precision\, recall\, F1 score\, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Grid Search: Use grid search to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters for the KNN model and identify the combination that results in the best performance. Hyperparameters to tune include n_neighbors\, metric\, and weights. Specific Grid Search Steps:

* Define a parameter grid:

param_grid = {'n_neighbors': [3\, 5\, 7\, 9]\, 'metric': ['euclidean'\, 'manhattan'\, 'minkowski']\, 'weights': ['uniform'\, 'distance']}

*

* Use GridSearchCV from Scikit-learn to perform the grid search:

GridSearchCV(estimator=KNeighborsClassifier()\, param_grid=param_grid\, cv=5\, scoring='accuracy'\, verbose=2\, n_jobs=-1)

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

Support Vector Machines
-----------------------

Support Vector Machines
-----------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Model Evaluation: Calculate metrics such as accuracy\, precision\, recall\, F1 score\, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Visualization (optional): Visualize the decision boundary (if applicable) to understand how the SVM separates the different classes.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Grid Search: Use grid search to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters for the SVM model and identify the combination that results in the best performance. Hyperparameters to tune include C\, kernel\, and gamma. Specific Grid Search Steps:

* Define a parameter grid:

param_grid = {'C': [0.1\, 1\, 10\, 100]\, 'gamma': [1\, 0.1\, 0.01\, 0.001]\, 'kernel': ['linear'\, 'rbf'\, 'poly']}

*

* Use GridSearchCV from Scikit-learn to perform the grid search:

GridSearchCV(estimator=SVC()\, param_grid=param_grid\, cv=5\, scoring='accuracy'\, verbose=2\, n_jobs=-1)

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

Decision Trees
--------------

Decision Trees
--------------

Evaluating the Model

* Bagging Implementation:

* Bagging Initialization: Use the BaggingClassifier from Scikit-learn\, wrapping the DecisionTreeClassifier. Specify the number of base estimators (n_estimators) and other relevant parameters.

* Model Fitting: Fit the Bagging model to the standardized Wine Quality dataset.

* Evaluation of Bagging Model: Evaluate the performance of the Bagging model using the same metrics as before and compare the results with the single decision tree model.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Model Evaluation: Calculate metrics such as accuracy\, precision\, recall\, F1 score\, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Visualization (optional): Visualize the decision tree structure to understand how decisions are made based on different features.

Random Forests
--------------

Random Forests
--------------

Evaluating the Model

* Model Evaluation: Calculate metrics such as accuracy\, precision\, recall\, F1 score\, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Feature Importance: Analyze the importance of each feature in making predictions by examining the feature importances attribute of the Random Forest model. You can access these by using the following:

#Get feature importances
importances = rf.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)[::-1]
feature_names = df.columns[:-1]

Tuning Model Parameters

Parameters:

#Number of trees in the forest
n_estimators = 100  

#Maximum depth of the tree
max_depth = None  

#Minimum number of samples required to split an internal node
min_samples_split = 2

#Number of features to consider when looking for the best split
max_features = 'sqrt'

* Grid Search: Use grid search to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters and identify the combination that results in the best performance. Hyperparameters to tune include n_estimators\, max_depth\, min_samples_split\, and max_features.

* Cross-Validation: Employ cross-validation during grid search to ensure that the hyperparameter tuning process is robust and the results are not biased by a single train-test split.

k-Means Clustering
------------------

k-Means Clustering
------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Cluster Assignment: After fitting the model\, obtain the cluster assignments for each data point.

* Evaluation Metrics:

* Inertia: Calculate the within-cluster sum of squares (inertia) to measure how tightly the clusters are packed.

* Silhouette Score: Use the silhouette score to evaluate the quality of the clusters. This score measures how similar a data point is to its own cluster compared to other clusters.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Choosing the Optimal K: Experiment with different numbers of clusters (K) and use methods like the Elbow Method and Silhouette Score to determine the optimal number of clusters.

* Elbow Method: Plot the inertia for different values of K and look for an “elbow” point where the inertia starts to decrease more slowly.

* Silhouette Score: Calculate the silhouette score for different values of K and choose the value that maximizes the score.

* Re-fitting the Model: After determining the optimal number of clusters\, refit the K-Means model using the selected K.

Gaussian Mixture Model
----------------------

Gaussian Mixture Model
----------------------

Evaluating the Model

Real-World Data: Evaluate the anomaly detection performance using metrics such as accuracy\, precision\, recall\, and confusion matrix.

Tuning Model Parameters

* Number of Components: Experiment with different numbers of Gaussian components to find the optimal value.

* Covariance Type: Test various covariance types and observe their impact on the model's performance.

* Techniques: Use methods like cross-validation to systematically explore the parameter space and identify the optimal settings.

Artificial Neural Networks
--------------------------

Artificial Neural Networks
--------------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Testing Data: Evaluate the model's performance on the testing dataset to assess its generalization capability.

* Performance Metrics: Calculate relevant metrics (e.g.\, accuracy\, precision\, recall for classification\; mean absolute error\, mean squared error for regression) to quantify the model's performance.

Tuning Model Parameters

Let’s try a few different models\, with Tensorflow you have the option to try different layers and all of their different parameters. Please do the following:

* Try at least 2 more model configurations

* For the configurations\, pick two layers and try different parameters for them.

* Answer the following

* How do the new models compare to the others\, what do you think causes the improvement or decay of the model performance?

* What parameters did you choose and why\, what was their overall impact on model performance?

Convolutional Neural Networks
-----------------------------

Convolutional Neural Networks
-----------------------------

Evaluating the Model

* Performance Metrics: Calculate metrics such as accuracy\, precision\, recall\, F1 score\, and confusion matrix to evaluate the model. These metrics provide insights into the model’s predictive performance and its ability to generalize.

* Loss and Accuracy Curves: Plot the loss and accuracy curves for the training and validation sets to understand the model’s learning process.

Tuning Model Parameters

Hyperparameter Tuning: Use techniques such as learning rate scheduling\, early stopping\, and model checkpointing to systematically explore a range of hyperparameters for the CNN model and identify the combination that results in the best performance.

Specific Tuning Steps:

*

Define a range of learning rates:

learning_rates = [0.001\, 0.0001\, 0.00001]

*

Implement early stopping to avoid overfitting:

EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss'\, patience=5)

*

Use model checkpointing to save the best model:

ModelCheckpoint(filepath='best_model.h5'\, monitor='val_loss'\, save_best_only=True)

Download Sample Images for Predictions (optional):

[rock_sample] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063843/download)      [paper sample] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063704/download)      [scissors sample] (https://elearning.mines.edu/courses/76742/files/8063819/download)

------------------------
SUBMITTING YOUR SOLUTION
------------------------

Submit your notebook file(s). To submit your notebook\, first clear all the cells (this won't matter too much this time\, but for larger data sets in the future\, it will make the file smaller). Then use the File->Download As->Notebook to obtain the notebook file. Finally\, submit the notebook file(s) with your solutions to Canvas.

Unit 5 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser\, hidden in app)

 

****
TASK
****

Your task is to complete the four steps below and participate in the in class discussion. This is a place for sharing ideas based on your research of another example/issue/case study of ethics in big data.

1. Watch the [Cathy O'Neil TED Talk] (https://www.ted.com/speakers/cathy_o_neil) 
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Note: If you would like to supplement the above TED Talk with some of Cathy O'Neil's\, here are some additional articles and such:

*

* Cathy O’Neil on the Unchecked Power of Algorithms ([interview] (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5lIGT5W1SWA) piece from Netflix’s The Social Dilemma Documentary)

* Algorithms that Run the World with Cathy O’Neil ([podcast] (https://thedecisionlab.com/podcasts/algorithms-that-run-the-world-with-cathy-oneil) from 2021)

* Ethics in Algorithms: Highlights from Cathy O’Neil’s Keynote at the Volunteer Summit ([article] (https://www.datakind.org/2021/10/06/ethics-in-algorithms-highlights-from-cathy-oneils-keynote-at-the-volunteer-summit/) from 2021)

* Or visit her [mathbabe.org] (https://mathbabe.org/) blog for lots of discussion over the last several years

2. Find Another Data Science Ethical Example
--------------------------------------------

Identify another example\, issue\, or case study related to ethics in big data. This could be from a news article\, an academic paper\, an organization’s controversial use of data\, or another reputable source.

3. Prepare a Case Study Paper
-----------------------------

Provide a one sentence explanation of your selected topic along with  your choice of a 1-2 page professionally written paper with references.  In this paper\,  summarize of the TED talk and what implications it has on your example/issue/case study of ethics in big data. This includes:

*

* Your example/issue/case study of ethics in big data

* Thoughts provoked by the Ted Talk and your example/issue/case study

* Pros / cons / opinons

GRADING
-------

This assignment is worth a total of 100 points.

assignments.title: Reading and Discussion: Advanced Visualization
assignments.description:
Unit 6 Progress

Icon Progress Bar/Navigation (built in browser\, hidden in app)

 

----
TASK
----

Your task for this discussion is to read one (ore more) of the following sections on advanced topics in visualizations in our Python Data Science Handbook. You will then post a discussion and a reply below! 

* Matplotlib:

* Multiple Subplots

* Text and Annotation

* Customizing Ticks

* Customizing Matplotlib: Configurations and Stylesheets

* Three-Dimensional Plotting in Matplotlib

* Visualization with Seaborn

* Other Python Visualization Libraries

* Bokeh

* Plotly

Reminder: The book is a reference guide and we have assigned the above section as a reading on this topic at a high-level. Keep in mind\, it contains much more information than we cover in our course. As time permits\, you may find some interesting "extra" readings\, examples\, and information.

-----------------------
DISCUSSION REQUIREMENTS
-----------------------

Once you have completed this weeks reading\, you are asked to complete the following tasks:

* Post a 1-2 sentence response from the reading for 2 of the three below prompts:

* Select a feature or idea that you would like to implement and a few sentences about how that would add value to your data storytelling / reporting of your final results.

* Select a library you have not used in the past (e.g.\, seaborn\, bokeh\, plotly\, other) and discuss what benefits it would have over that of the basic matplotlib.

* Provide a free response: this can be anything from clarification questions\, something that piqued your interest\, or may be a personal experience you have with what was discussed in the book section.

* Post a meaningful reply to another student's question/post. 

Our goal is to help you get a deeper understanding of data science and discussions are a wonderful way to do this! Think of this as a learning opportunity for both you\, your classmates\, and your professor! Be sure to use academic language and question ideas from the readings for clarity or to push your own thinking.  You may of course respond to more than two of the questions and more of your classmates if you would like.

-------
GRADING
-------

This assignment is worth a total of 15 points this is split into 10 points for a thoughtful post and 5 points for a thoughtful reply.  Once you post your answers to your selected questions\, you will be able to see your peer's responses. This discussion requires you to post first before seeing other responses. To see the specific grading criteria for this assignment\, please [view the discussion rubric] (https://community.canvaslms.com/docs/DOC-10577-4212540120).
